title: skopeo copy to Nexus Proxy Repository (404 Error)
body:
Currently, using Nexus Repository with some proxy repositories, I would like to utilize skopeo copy to copy a container image from a public registry to a Docker proxy repository configured in Nexus. This is particularly useful for Windows container images, as it eliminates the need for a Windows environment solely to fetch Windows container images.

However, with proxy repositories, uploading artifacts is not possible, which makes skopeo copy fail with a "404 Not Found" error. As mentioned in the Skopeo documentation, this tool should be adapted for air-gapped environments, but the setup I’ve described doesn’t work, even though this is a basic and necessary feature for such environments

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out.

It’s rather unclear to me what is the problem here. The typical report structure I’d expect is “steps to reproduce / expected results / actual results”.

Are you saying that an upload should write to mirrors? That doesn’t really work if there are multiple mirrors — it turns the operation into an update in a distributed system, not something that can be just run from a command line without the mirrors being explicitly aware of each other in a cluster with fencing / locking and the like.

The thing that Skopeo could do, and that _might_ be relevant to you, is to copy directly to a mirror — explicitly use the mirror as a destination. Of course, that leaves the synchronization / propagation of the write up to other tools — the distributed system task does not really go away.

Thank you for your response. **Steps to reproduce** **Environment:** - OS: Ubuntu 25.04 (Plucky) - Skoepo version: 1.13.3 - Nexus version: 3.80.0 **Context:** On a fresh Nexus deployment, I created a proxy repository that mirrors the Microsoft container registry (mcr.microsoft.com). **Actual results** After running the following command to copy a Windows image from MCR to the proxy repository: `skopeo copy --override-os windows docker://mcr.microsoft.com/windows/nanoserver:ltsc2025-amd64 docker://:/nanoserver:ltsc2025-amd64 --dest-tls-verify=false --dest-creds : --debug` I get the following logs: Same behavior with Linux image. **Expected results** The command should successfully copy the Windows image from MCR to the proxy repository. **Note:** When I run the same command targeting a hosted repository instead of a proxy one, it works as expected. As a reminder, in most repository managers, proxy (or mirror) repositories are not designed to accept direct uploads. They are meant to cache artifacts on-demand when users retrieve them (e.g., via docker pull). Regarding scenarios with multiple mirrors or high-availability deployments, I believe this is outside the scope of the skopeo tool.

Thanks!

```
POST http://…/v2/nanoserver/blobs/uploads/
```
… receives a HTTP 404, with a HTML content. That is the destination registry outright not handling attempts to write, and _not following the registry error reporting protocol_.

Substantially, this must be fixed server-side; there is nothing Skopeo can do to upload to a server that doesn’t accept uploads.

As for the error reporting, I think the error does contain all of the relevant information (URL, HTTP status, indication of HTML content), although the formatting could be improved — e.g. “StatusCode” does not say that it is a HTTP status, although I think that was understood in this context.

Hi,

Thanks for your response.

The issue is that all the different repository managers (Nexus, Artifactory, Harbour, etc.) work the same when it comes to proxy/mirror repositories, which means it’s not possible to upload an artefact manually.

I think Skopeo should have a feature that behaves similarly to pulling an image (docker pull, podman pull). As it stands, it seems that my use case isn’t covered by the tool.

Perhaps you could update the documentation of Skopeo to explain this current limitation. Because, when we read sentences about using skopeo in air-gapped environment, we might assume that this use case should work.

If you are saying that the mirror fetches an image on demand, and that pulling an image from the mirror would cause it to be fetched
- I don’t understand how that relates to a `skopeo copy` writing to a mirror. That’s just not the same thing.
- Of course it is possible to do, e.g., `skopeo copy docker://$mirror dir:$tmp && rm -rf $tmp`. Or maybe just a `skopeo inspect` could trigger things, I wouldn’t know.

---

>  Because, when we read sentences about using skopeo in air-gapped environment

Any particular documentation that needs updating?

Thank you,

The skopeo copy command works when specifying a local folder as the destination. It triggers a pull that caches the fetched container image.

From the README file:

"Syncing an external image repository to an internal registry for air-gapped deployments."

And from the official Red Hat page (https://www.redhat.com/en/topics/containers/what-is-skopeo):

"Skopeo also allows you to delete an image from a repository and sync an external image repository to an internal registry for more secure disconnected network (also known as air-gapped) deployments."
I read those as referring to a (full-featured) “internal registry” which supports both writes and reads; not to specifically read-only pull-through mirrors.
